questions.main_text:
Smile for the Camera

â

**Read the text carefully and for each item choose the alternative that best fits the context. (7 p.)**

â

 Today when someone points a camera 1) [SELECT id=128] us, we smile. Such are our expectations of a portrait. But, as a walk around any art gallery reveals, the open smile has been largely, so it seems, frowned upon.

â

Itâs commonly thought that for centuries people didnât smile in pictures because their teeth were awful. This is not true â bad teeth were so common that this was not seen as necessarily 2) [SELECT id=129] from a personâs attractiveness. Lord Palmerston, the then prime minister, was described as being devastatingly good-looking, despite his having 3) [SELECT id=130] good reasons 4) [SELECT id=131]

â

The primary reason for not smiling? Itâs hard to do. In the few examples we have of smiles in formal portraiture, the effect is often not very pleasing, and we can still see this today. When a camera is produced, we perform gamely. But 5) [SELECT id=132] take too long, our smiles become grimaces. A smile is like a blush â a response, not an expression, and it can 6) [SELECT id=133] recorded.

â

Smiling also has many cultural significances, few in line with our modern perceptions of it as a physical signal of enjoyment. Earlier in Europe it was a well-established fact that the only people who smiled broadly were 7) [SELECT id=134] . Showing teeth was, for the upper class, a breach of etiquette.

questions.main_text:
The Link between Dreams and Faith



Read the text carefully and for each item choose the alternative that best fits the context. (7 p.)



Itâs easy to assume that dreams are 1) [SELECT id=121] neural nonsense: series of random scenes and images, with few discernible health benefits, that 2) [SELECT id=122] us while we sleep. But modern neuroscience tells a different story â namely, that dreams can prime us to be spiritual, in one form or 3) [SELECT id=123] .



To understand why, it helps to understand what happens to our bodies when we dream. Though we may not be physically conscious during sleep, 4) [SELECT id=124] our brains remain extremely active, especially in the networks responsible 5) [SELECT id=125] visual imaginations, emotional memories and instinctual responses.



These are the elements that characterize 6) [SELECT id=126] psychologist C. G. Jung called âbig dreamsâ; intense, memorable experiences such as magical flying, visitations from the dead, nightmares of demonic attack and lucid dreams of higher consciousness. And such dreams are fairly common.



Science may not be able to prove the existence of God or the gods. But it can prove that, for many people, dreams may offer a way to expand their sense of reality and attain a higher level of being, 7) [SELECT id=127] religion.

But modern neuroscience tells a different story â namely, that dreams can prime us to be spiritual, in one form or 3) [SELECT id=123] .



To understand why, it helps to understand what happens to our bodies when we dream. Though we may not be physically conscious during sleep, 4) [SELECT id=124] our brains remain extremely active, especially in the networks responsible 5) [SELECT id=125] visual imaginations, emotional memories and instinctual responses.



These are the elements that characterize 6) [SELECT id=126] psychologist C. G. Jung called âbig dreamsâ; intense, memorable experiences such as magical flying, visitations from the dead, nightmares of demonic attack and lucid dreams of higher consciousness. And such dreams are fairly common.



Science may not be able to prove the existence of God or the gods. But it can prove that, for many people, dreams may offer a way to expand their sense of reality and attain a higher level of being, 7) [SELECT id=127] religion.

questions.main_text:
Step inside NYCâs Oldest Bookstore

â

Read the text carefully and then answer the question in Finnish.

â

New York City has an array of eclectic independent bookstores. Littered throughout the city, each has its own unique history and facts, but Argosy Books holds the title of oldest bookstore in New York City. The items at Argosy can be purchased individually, but they also sell Books by the Foot for decorating purposes to interior decorators, model homes and the like. Though more professional companies purchase books this way, they can also be purchased by the foot by those who simply want a library scheme for their own homes. As an antique bookseller, Argosy caters not only to readers, but also to people who desire what books are made of: the paper, its age, covers, spines, binding, condition and such.

â

Mihin ilmaus âbooks by the footâ viittaa, ja kenelle siitÃĊ on iloa? Vastaa lyhyesti suomeksi. (6 p.)

New York City has an array of eclectic independent bookstores. Littered throughout the city, each has its own unique history and facts, but Argosy Books holds the title of oldest bookstore in New York City. The items at Argosy can be purchased individually, but they also sell Books by the Foot for decorating purposes to interior decorators, model homes and the like. Though more professional companies purchase books this way, they can also be purchased by the foot by those who simply want a library scheme for their own homes. As an antique bookseller, Argosy caters not only to readers, but also to people who desire what books are made of: the paper, its age, covers, spines, binding, condition and such.
